nameMeanings.name: Austin
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English, French, and German: from the personal name Austin, from Latin Augustinus, a derivative of Augustus (see Augustin ). This was an extremely common personal name in every part of Western Europe during the Middle Ages, owing its popularity chiefly to Saint Augustine of Hippo (354–430), whose influence on Christianity is generally considered to be second only to that of Saint Paul. Various religious orders came to be formed following rules named in his honor, including the ‘Austin canons’, established in the 11th century, and the ‘Austin friars’, a mendicant order dating from the 13th century. The popularity of the personal name in England was further increased by the fact that it was borne by Saint Augustine of Canterbury (died c. 605), an Italian Benedictine monk known as ‘the Apostle of the English’, who brought Christianity to southern England in 597 and founded the see of Canterbury.
English: variant of   Aspden  , with which this surname became confused.

History: This was the name of a merchant family that became established in eastern MA in the 17th century, notably in Charlestown. Richard Austin came from England and landed at Boston in 1638, and his son Anthony was clerk of Suffield, CT, in 1674. The surname is very common in England as well as America; this Richard Austin was only one of a number of bearers who brought it to North America. — In 1821 Stephen F. Austin (1793–1836), born in Austinville VA, founded the first Anglo colony in TX.

variants.name: Augustine
meanings: Americanized form of any of various European surnames from personal names derived from Latin Augustinus (see Austin ), for example German, Dutch, French, and Czech Augustin 1, Dutch Augustijn, Polish Augustyn , Slovenian Avguštin or Auguštin (see Augustin 2), Italian Agostino .
History: This surname is listed in the register of Huguenot ancestors recognized by the Huguenot Society of America.

variants.name: Agostino
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rocco, Salvatore, Antonio, Nicola, Vincenzo, Carlo, Carmelo, Cono, Cosmo, Domenico, Elio, Enzo, Lino, Armando, Arturo, Atilio, Octavio, Silvana.
Italian: from the personal name Agostino, from Latin Augustinus (see Austin ).

variants.name: Agoston
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Hungarian Tibor, Attila, Csaba, Gabor, Geza, Istvan, Jeno, Laszlo.
Hungarian (Ágoston): from the personal name Ágoston, from Latin Augustinus (see Austin ). This surname is also common in Romania.

variants.name: Auston
meanings: English:
variant of   Austin  .
variant of   Owston  .

variants.name: Augenstein
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Helmut, Manfred.
German: from an altered form of the personal name Augustin (see Austin ).

variants.name: Augustin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Andre, Fernand, Jacques, Jean Claude, Monique, Renold, Andree, Antoine, Arsene, Celine, Dany.  German Kurt, Fritz, Gunter, Hans, Erwin, Georg, Gerda, Inge, Juergen, Klaus, Matthias, Otto.

French, West Indian (mainly Haiti), German, Dutch, and Czech; Slovak (mainly Augustín); Croatian (also Auguštin): from the personal name Augustin, Slovak Augustín, from Latin Augustinus (see Austin ). In North America, this surname may also be a shortened form of the patronymics and other derivatives of these personal names, such as Slovak Augustiňák. This surname is by far most common in Haiti.

Americanized form of Slovenian Avguštin or Auguštin: from the personal name Avguštin, from Latin Augustinus (see Austin ). In North America, this surname may also be a shortened and altered form of the Slovenian patronymics like Avguštinčič. Compare Augustine .

variants.name: Agustin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Conrado, Ernesto, Renato, Rodolfo, Salvador, Antonio, Carlos, Francisco, Manuel, Adela, Alfredo, Alicia.
Spanish (Agustín): from the personal name Agustín, from Latin Augustinus (see Austin ).

variants.name: Augustus
meanings: Dutch, German, English, Welsh, West Indian (mainly Trinidad and Tobago), and African (mainly Nigeria): from the Latin personal name Augustus (from the adjective augustus ‘consecrated, majestic, venerable’), or a Latinized (humanistic) form of German and English August . It was the Emperor Augustus (63 BC – AD 14), after whom it became conventional for Roman emperors to adopt Augustus as a title on their accession and in whose honor the month of August was named. The personal name became popular among early Christians, who read into it the implication that the bearer had become greater by being baptized. The surname Augustus is virtually non-existing in Germany.

variants.name: Austen
meanings: English: variant of   Austin  , associated chiefly with southeastern England, especially Kent.
German: from a shortened form of the personal name   Augustin  .

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The Royal Exchange is Organized
historicEvents.description: The Royal Exchange was organized in January 1571 by Sir Thomas Gresham. Gresham is known as the father of English banking.

historicEvents.title: Elizabeth I Signs Death Warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots
historicEvents.description: Elizabeth I signed the death warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots, on February 1, 1587, as she was found guilty of plotting to assasinate Elizabeth. Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England on February 8, 1587.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Elizabeth I.org - Mary Queen of Scots

historicEvents.title: The Honourable East India Company
historicEvents.description: The Honourable East India Company, a British joint-stock company, was established in 1600 for trade in the Indian Ocean region. At its height, the British East India Company had a private army which was twice the size of the British Army, ruled large sections of India, and revenues in the millions.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Historic UK - The East India Company

historicEvents.title: The Pilgrims Board the Mayflower
historicEvents.description: "The Pilgrims boarded the Mayflower in search of religious freedom in the New World in September 1620. The Mayflower set sail from Plymouth, England, with 102 passengers, 40 of whom called themselves ""Saints."" After two stormy months at sea, the ship reached the New World. Nearly half of the group died during their first winter due to malnutrition and the harsh New England winter."

en.lifeSummary: When Richard Austin I was born on 28 February 1547, in Titchfield, Hampshire, England, his father, William Austin, was 31 and his mother, Alice Littlefield, was 27. He married Anne on 20 December 1585, in St Peter's Church, Titchfield, Hampshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 3 sons and 5 daughters. He died on 15 February 1623, in Winchester, Hampshire, England, at the age of 75, and was buried in Titchfield, Hampshire, England.

Il a épousé Anne le 20 décembre 1585, à St Peter's Church, Titchfield, Hampshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 3 fils et 5 filles. Il est décédé le 15 février 1623, à Winchester, Hampshire, Angleterre, à l’âge de 75 ans, et a été enterré à Titchfield, Hampshire, Angleterre.

ko.St Peter's Church, Titchfield, Hampshire: 영국 잉글랜드 함프실르 티트ch필드 스트 피터'스 처르치
ko.lifeSummary: Richard Austin I(이)가 1547년 2월 28일 영국 함프실르 티트ch필드에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 William Austin(은)는 31세였고, 그의 어머니 Alice Littlefield(은)는 27세였다. 그는 1585년 12월 20일 영국 잉글랜드 함프실르 티트ch필드 스트 피터'스 처르치에서 Anne(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 세 명의 아들과 다섯 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1623년 2월 15일 영국 함프실르 윈체스터에서 75세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 함프실르 티트ch필드에 묻혔다.

Он женился на женщине по имени Anne 20 декабря 1585 г. в местности ст питир'с чурч, тичфиелд, хампшир, Англия, Великобритания. У них было по крайней мере 3 сына и 5 дочерей. Он умер 15 февраля 1623 г. в местности винчестир, хампшир, Англия в возрасте 75 лет и был похоронен в местности тичфиелд, хампшир, Англия.
